title: Micropayment authorization circuit for agent commerce
description: Add a PaymentAuthorization circuit that extends delegation with spend limits. The delegatee proves it has permission to spend up to N tokens on behalf of the delegator, with cumulative spend tracked via a running nullifier chain. This is the missing primitive for agentic commerce — without it, every agent transaction requires human approval, killing the autonomous UX. Wire format: Poseidon4(spendCap, cumulativeSpent, delegateeCredCommitment, merchantScope). Verifier checks cumulativeSpent <= spendCap and the chain links to a valid delegation proof.

title: Reusable session tokens from handshake proofs
description: After a successful handshake, emit a short-lived session token (EIP-712 signed struct containing humanNullifier, agentNullifier, scopeCommitment, expiry) that downstream services can verify without re-running the full ZK verification. Currently every interaction requires a fresh proof (~2-5s generation time), which is a non-starter for real-time commerce flows like checkout or API call chains. Session tokens reduce per-request cost to a single ecrecover while preserving the trust guarantees of the initial handshake.

title: OpenAI function-calling and MCP tool adapter
description: Build a thin adapter that wraps Bolyra handshake + delegation as an OpenAI-compatible function/tool definition and as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. This lets any agent framework that supports function calling or MCP — GPT-4, Claude, LangChain, CrewAI — use Bolyra auth with zero custom integration. The adapter handles proof generation, session management, and scope negotiation behind a single `authenticate_with_bolyra()` tool. This is the wedge for ecosystem adoption: meet developers where they already are.

title: On-chain scope registry for discoverable agent capabilities
description: Deploy a ScopeRegistry contract where service providers register named scopes (e.g., 'stripe:charge:usd', 'github:repo:write') with human-readable metadata and required permission bits. Currently scope bitmasks are opaque 64-bit values with no discoverability — agents and developers have no way to know what scopes exist or what bits to request. The registry maps string identifiers to bit positions, enabling agents to programmatically discover and negotiate capabilities. This is table stakes for a multi-vendor agentic commerce ecosystem.

title: Agent credential rotation without tree re-enrollment
description: Add a RotateCredential circuit that proves knowledge of an existing enrolled credential and a valid operator signature over the new credential, outputting both old and new commitments. The contract atomically removes the old leaf and inserts the new one. Currently, key rotation requires revoking and re-enrolling, which invalidates all in-flight proofs and breaks active delegation chains. For production commerce — where agents run continuously and key rotation is a compliance requirement — seamless rotation is essential.

title: W3C DID Method Specification for did:bolyra
description: Define a complete did:bolyra DID method spec mapping identity commitments to did:bolyra:<hex(commitment)>. Specify the DID Document structure (verification methods using BabyJubJub keys, service endpoints for handshake initiation), the resolution algorithm (on-chain lookup via IdentityRegistry), CRUD operations (create = enroll, deactivate = revoke), and a Verifiable Credential schema for handshake attestations. Without a DID binding, Bolyra identities are siloed and cannot interoperate with the W3C Verifiable Credentials ecosystem or existing SSI wallets.

title: ABNF wire format grammar for proof payloads
description: Author a machine-parseable ABNF grammar (RFC 5234) for all protocol wire messages: enrollment requests, handshake proof bundles (human proof + agent proof + optional delegation chain), public signal arrays, and delegation tokens. Currently the only serialization is implicit in the TypeScript SDK and Solidity ABI — a second implementor would have to reverse-engineer field ordering, encoding, and length prefixes. An ABNF grammar makes the protocol implementable from spec alone, which is a precondition for any IETF standardization track.

title: Scope bitmask registry with IANA-style governance
description: Define an extensible registry for the 64-bit permission bitmask namespace. Bits 0-15 should be protocol-reserved (read, write, financial tiers per the cumulative encoding invariant), bits 16-47 application-defined via a first-come-first-served registry, and bits 48-63 private-use. Without a registry, independent implementations will collide on bit assignments, breaking cross-deployment interoperability. The spec should include the registration template, change controller rules, and an initial assignments table aligned with the current cumulative encoding (bit 2/3/4 hierarchy).

title: NIST SP 800-63B authenticator assurance level mapping
description: Produce a formal mapping of Bolyra's authentication factors to NIST AAL levels. HumanUniqueness (EdDSA key + Merkle membership) maps naturally to a single-factor cryptographic authenticator (AAL1); adding the agent mutual handshake as a second factor could reach AAL2. Document the gap analysis: what's missing for AAL3 (hardware-bound key, verifier impersonation resistance), and how the planned platform attestation circuit (TPM/SEV) would close it. This artifact is essential for credit union adoption since NCUA examiners evaluate against NIST guidelines.

title: Interoperability profile with mandatory/optional feature tiers
description: Define three conformance tiers using RFC 2119 language: Core (MUST: handshake verify, enrollment, root history checks), Extended (SHOULD: delegation chains up to depth 3, scope commitment verification), and Full (MAY: cross-chain root sync, off-chain batching). Each tier specifies the exact set of circuits, contract interfaces, and wire format messages an implementation must support. Pair this with the existing conformance test vectors, tagging each vector with its required tier. This prevents fragmentation where partial implementations claim Bolyra compatibility without supporting critical security properties like nullifier checking.

title: Python SDK with native snarkjs bridge for LangChain/CrewAI
description: Ship a `bolyra` PyPI package that wraps snarkjs via a lightweight Node subprocess or WASM bridge, exposing `enroll_agent()`, `prove_handshake()`, `verify_handshake()`, and `delegate()` as sync/async Python callables. The SDK must return Pydantic models so LangChain's structured output parsing and CrewAI's task results can consume proof artifacts directly. Without this, every Python agent framework user has to hand-roll subprocess calls to snarkjs and parse JSON outputs manually — a dealbreaker for adoption.

title: LangChain BaseTool wrapper with automatic proof caching
description: Build a `BolyraAuthTool(BaseTool)` that agents invoke before sensitive operations. The tool takes a session nonce, generates an AgentPolicy proof, and returns a structured `AuthResult` with nullifier, scope commitment, and TTL. Include a `ProofCache` that skips re-proving if a valid proof exists for the current scope+nonce window. This maps directly to LangChain's tool-calling pattern — agents already call tools before actions, so auth becomes one more tool in the chain rather than middleware the developer has to wire up.

title: CrewAI task delegation → Bolyra scope-narrowing bridge
description: CrewAI's `Task.delegate()` passes work from one agent to another with natural-language context but zero cryptographic enforcement. Build a `BolyraCrewAIPlugin` that intercepts delegation events, maps CrewAI role permissions to Bolyra's 64-bit scope bitmask (e.g., 'researcher' → read-only bits, 'executor' → write bits), generates a Delegation circuit proof, and attaches the `newScopeCommitment` to the delegated task's context. The delegatee agent can then present this proof to any verifier. This turns CrewAI's trust-on-vibes delegation into cryptographically enforced scope narrowing.

title: OpenAI-compatible HTTP middleware for framework-agnostic auth
description: Most agent frameworks (AutoGen, LangChain, CrewAI, custom) ultimately make HTTP calls to LLM APIs via OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Build a lightweight HTTPX/aiohttp middleware that intercepts outbound LLM calls, attaches a Bolyra proof as a custom header (`X-Bolyra-Proof`), and verifies incoming response proofs. This gives every framework zero-code ZK auth without needing per-framework integrations. Ship as both a Python `httpx.Auth` plugin and a standalone proxy binary for non-Python frameworks like AutoGen's .NET variant.

title: Standardized scope bitmask registry for agent frameworks
description: The current 64-bit `permissionBitmask` is powerful but meaningless without a shared mapping from bits to capabilities. Define and publish a `bolyra-scopes.json` registry that maps bit positions to canonical agent capabilities (bit 0 = read-public, bit 1 = read-private, bit 2 = write, bit 3 = execute, bit 4 = delegate, etc.) with cumulative encoding rules already enforced by the circuit. Without this, every framework integration will invent its own bit assignments and interop breaks immediately. Include a `ScopeBuilder` utility class in both TS and Python SDKs.

title: Enforce delegatee expiry ≤ delegator expiry in Delegation circuit
description: The Delegation circuit declares delegateeExpiry and delegatorExpiry as private inputs and comments state 'must be <= delegatorExpiry', but the truncated circuit source shows no LessThan constraint enforcing this. Without an explicit `LessThan(64)` component comparing delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry, a malicious delegatee can set an expiry far beyond the delegator's, escaping temporal scope narrowing. Add a `LessThan(64)` gadget and assert the output is 1. Write a negative test vector where delegateeExpiry > delegatorExpiry and confirm the circuit rejects it.

title: Add domain separation tags to all Poseidon nullifier hashes
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret), AgentPolicy computes Poseidon2(sessionNonce, credentialCommitment), and Delegation computes its own nullifier. All use the same Poseidon2 hash with no domain separator. If a scope value collides with a sessionNonce value, a human nullifier and an agent nullifier could collide, enabling cross-circuit nullifier confusion attacks. Prepend a unique domain tag constant (e.g., 0 for human, 1 for agent, 2 for delegation) as the first Poseidon input, upgrading to Poseidon3. Update the IETF draft's Security Considerations section and add cross-circuit collision test vectors.

title: Blind scope commitments against bitmask brute-force
description: AgentPolicy outputs scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). The permissionBitmask is 64 bits, giving only 2^64 preimage candidates — feasible for a motivated attacker with GPU clusters to brute-force given a known credentialCommitment format. Add a random blinding salt as a third Poseidon input: Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt). The salt is a private input drawn uniformly from the field. Mirror this change in the Delegation circuit's previousScopeCommitment verification. Write a formal property: for any two distinct salts, the probability of scopeCommitment collision is negligible.

title: Certora-style invariant properties for all three circuits
description: Write machine-checkable invariant specifications for HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, and Delegation. Properties include: (1) no private input can exceed its declared bit-width after Num2Bits decomposition, (2) nullifierHash is deterministic given (scope, secret) or (nonce, credCommitment), (3) scopeCommitment chain is monotonically narrowing across delegation hops (delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0), (4) Merkle root output is uniquely determined by leaf + proof path. Express these in a property specification language (circomspect annotations or a standalone JSON property file) that can be consumed by circomspect or picus. Include 10+ negative witness test vectors that violate each property.

title: Constrain merkleProofLength to prevent depth manipulation
description: All three circuits pass merkleProofLength as a private input to BinaryMerkleRoot. If the BinaryMerkleRoot template does not internally constrain that merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH, an attacker could supply a depth of 0 and make any leaf value equal the 'root', bypassing membership checks entirely. Audit the @zk-kit/binary-merkle-root implementation for this bound check. If absent, add an explicit RangeProof: LessThan(8) asserting merkleProofLength >= 1 AND merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH. Add test vectors for depth=0 and depth=MAX_DEPTH+1 to the conformance suite.

title: Cross-chain root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base deployment. Implement a RootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs of humanRootHistory/agentRootHistory slots from the source chain, verified against the source chain's state root available via each L2's L1 block oracle. This avoids bridges entirely — any relayer can post a storage proof and the destination chain verifies it trustlessly. Add a `foreignRootExists` mapping and extend `handshakeVerify` to accept roots from any registered source chain.

title: Chain-agnostic nullifier registry to prevent cross-chain replay
description: The current nullifier check (`usedNonces`, `usedNullifiers`) is per-contract, meaning a handshake proof verified on Base can be replayed on Arbitrum if the same root is synced. Add a `chainId` field to the nullifier derivation: `nullifier = Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId)` in HumanUniqueness and `Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chainId)` in AgentPolicy. This makes nullifiers chain-specific at the circuit level. Publish a shared nullifier Bloom filter commitment on-chain that cross-chain relayers can reference for probabilistic double-spend detection before full finality.

title: Multi-chain provider abstraction in @bolyra/sdk
description: Extend the existing TypeScript SDK with a `ChainProvider` interface that abstracts chain-specific RPC calls, gas estimation, and contract addresses. Ship pre-configured providers for Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon with automatic chain detection from the connected wallet. Add `sdk.verifyHandshake({ preferredChain: 'arbitrum' })` that selects the cheapest chain with a fresh root. This is the critical adoption path — dApp developers should not need to know which chain holds the current root.

title: Cross-chain delegation relay via Chainlink CCIP
description: A delegation chain may start on Base (where the delegator's agent is enrolled) but the delegatee operates on Polygon. Implement a DelegationRelay contract that accepts a verified delegation proof on the source chain and emits a CCIP message carrying the newScopeCommitment and delegateeMerkleRoot to the destination chain. The destination IdentityRegistry accepts this as a valid chain link, enabling the delegatee to continue the delegation chain or complete a handshake on their home chain without re-proving. Cap cross-chain hops at 1 per delegation chain to bound latency.

title: Cross-chain test vectors in conformance suite
description: Extend the existing conformance test suite with 10+ vectors covering multi-chain scenarios: valid storage proof relay, stale foreign root rejection, cross-chain nullifier replay (must fail), delegation chain spanning two chains, and chainId mismatch in nullifier derivation. Each vector should include the source chainId, destination chainId, and the storage proof or CCIP payload. This ensures any new chain deployment can validate interop correctness before going live.

title: Reduce Poseidon arity in AgentPolicy credential hash
description: AgentPolicy uses Poseidon(5) for the credential commitment (~1500 constraints for t=6 Poseidon). Split into two cascaded Poseidon(2) calls: h1=Poseidon2(modelHash, operatorPubkeyAx), h2=Poseidon2(operatorPubkeyAy, permissionBitmask), final=Poseidon2(h1, Poseidon2(h2, expiryTimestamp)). Three Poseidon(2) calls at ~300 constraints each = ~900 total, saving ~600 constraints. Also makes the commitment scheme compatible with binary Merkle tree internal hashing, simplifying future recursive composition.

title: Reuse bitmask Num2Bits decomposition for scope subset check
description: In Delegation.circom, delegatorScope and delegateeScope each get Num2Bits(64) for range checks, then the subset check (delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0) likely decomposes again. The bit arrays from range checks should be reused directly for the AND/NOT bitwise subset verification, eliminating a redundant 128-constraint decomposition. Concretely: compute subset = SUM(delegateeBits[i] * (1 - delegatorBits[i])) === 0 using the existing bit arrays, and also reuse delegateeBits for the cumulative encoding invariant check (bit4 implies bit2+bit3).

title: Replace EdDSA verify with Poseidon-HMAC credential binding
description: EdDSA verification in AgentPolicy costs ~30k constraints — over 60% of the circuit budget. If the operator is the one generating the proof (common in agent scenarios), replace EdDSA with a secret-key commitment scheme: store commitment = Poseidon(operatorSecret, credentialCommitment) in the tree, and prove knowledge of operatorSecret. This drops ~30k constraints to ~300, bringing AgentPolicy from ~50k to ~20k constraints and cutting proving time by roughly 50%. EdDSA is only needed when the prover differs from the signer; add a boolean mode signal to select between the two paths using Mux1.

title: Migrate HumanUniqueness from Groth16 to PLONK for unified proving
description: HumanUniqueness uses Groth16 (requiring trusted setup ceremony reuse from Semaphore v4 depth-20), while AgentPolicy and Delegation use PLONK. This forces SDK consumers to bundle two different proving systems, increasing WASM bundle size by ~2MB and doubling key management complexity. Migrate HumanUniqueness to PLONK with the same universal SRS. The circuit is small (~15k constraints with BabyPbk + Merkle + Poseidon), well within PLONK's sweet spot. Unifies the verifier interface, halves client-side code, and removes the ceremony dependency.

title: Add Merkle depth bounds check to prevent over-sized proof padding
description: All three circuits use BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH) with MAX_DEPTH=20, but merkleProofLength is unconstrained beyond being a signal. If merkleProofLength > actual tree depth, the prover pads with zero siblings, which still produces a valid root for a different (larger) tree. Add an explicit constraint: merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH via LessEqThan(5), and on-chain verify the root matches the registry's current tree size. This costs ~10 constraints but closes a subtle soundness gap where a proof for a depth-5 tree could be replayed against a depth-20 tree with a crafted root.

title: Unified top-level SDK facade with <10 line hello-world
description: The current SDK exposes only a low-level DelegationProver class buried under @bolyra/sdk/delegation/prover. Developers must separately handle HumanUniqueness proofs, AgentPolicy proofs, and Delegation proofs with no unifying entry point. Create a single top-level facade: `createIdentity()`, `enrollHuman()`, `enrollAgent()`, `proveHandshake()`, `verifyHandshake()`, `delegate()` — all exported from `@bolyra/sdk`. Include a `BolyraClient` class that wraps provider config, circuit paths, and registry address so developers configure once and call methods. Target: a complete human-agent handshake in under 10 lines of code.

title: Structured error hierarchy with recovery hints
description: The SDK currently throws bare `new Error(message)` strings, and the contracts use custom Solidity errors like `UnknownAgentRoot` that don't map to SDK-layer exceptions. Introduce a `BolyraError` base class with typed subclasses: `StaleRootError` (includes which root and how old), `RevokedIdentityError`, `ScopeMismatchError`, `ExpiredCredentialError`, `ReplayDetectedError`. Each error should carry a `code` string, the failing `publicSignals`, and a `recoveryHint` field (e.g., 'Re-fetch the agent tree root and retry'). Map all Solidity custom errors to their SDK counterparts in the verification flow.

title: Fluent credential and delegation builder API
description: Building circuit inputs currently requires manually constructing BigInt arrays for permission bitmasks, computing Poseidon hashes for credential commitments, and assembling Merkle proof objects. Introduce fluent builders: `Credential.agent().model('gpt-4').permissions(['read', 'write_10k']).expiresIn('30d').sign(operatorKey)` and `Delegation.from(agentCredential).to(delegateeCommitment).narrowScope(['read']).expiresIn('7d').sign(delegatorKey)`. The builders enforce cumulative bit encoding invariants (bit 4 implies bits 2+3) at construction time and produce the exact witness input the circuits expect.

title: LangChain BolyraAuthTool with structured output
description: LangChain/LangGraph is the dominant AI agent framework. Build a `BolyraAuthTool` extending `StructuredTool` that wraps `proveHandshake()` and `delegate()`. The tool accepts natural-language-friendly inputs (scope as string array, delegation target as agent name) and returns structured output with proof status, granted permissions, and chain depth. Include a `BolyraGuard` runnable that gates downstream tool execution on successful handshake verification — agents cannot call sensitive tools without a valid proof. Ship as `@bolyra/langchain` with a working example of a ReAct agent that authenticates before accessing a protected API.

title: CLI proof inspector and debug dashboard
description: When a proof fails verification, developers currently get a boolean `false` with no diagnostic information. Build `npx @bolyra/cli inspect <proof.json>` that decodes public signals into human-readable fields (scope bits as named permissions, timestamps as ISO dates, Merkle root with on-chain validity status), checks each verification step independently (signature valid? root in history? nullifier fresh? scope subset?), and prints a colored diff showing exactly which constraint failed. Also add `npx @bolyra/cli tree-status --provider <rpc>` to show current tree sizes, latest roots, and root history buffer occupancy for both humanTree and agentTree.

title: BabyJubJub secret scalar range constraint missing in HumanUniqueness
description: HumanUniqueness.circom feeds `secret` directly into both BabyPbk (scalar multiplication on Baby Jubjub, subgroup order l ≈ 2^251) and Poseidon(scope, secret) for nullifier derivation. If secret₁ ≡ secret₂ (mod l) but secret₁ ≠ secret₂ (mod p, the BN128 scalar field), both produce the same public key (same identity commitment, same Merkle leaf) but distinct nullifiers. One human identity can therefore generate multiple valid proofs per scope, completely breaking sybil resistance. Fix: add `component secretRange = Num2Bits(251); secretRange.in <== secret;` plus a LessThan check against the Baby Jubjub subgroup order constant, immediately after the secret input declaration.

title: Scope commitment is brute-forceable due to missing blinding salt
description: In AgentPolicy.circom, scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). The credentialCommitment is a public Merkle leaf (observable from enrollment events), and permissionBitmask is only 64 bits — trivially brute-forceable (2^64 is large but feasible for a motivated attacker with precomputation tables, and real-world bitmasks occupy a far smaller space). This leaks the agent's exact permission set from the public scopeCommitment, undermining the privacy model where scope bits are supposed to be private. Fix: add a random 253-bit blinding salt as a private input and compute scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt). Propagate the same change to the Delegation circuit's scope chain linking.

title: Operator-controlled root history buffer eviction enables denial of service
description: IdentityRegistry.sol uses a 30-slot ring buffer for both agentRootHistory and humanRootHistory. The owner (operator) can call enrollAgent/enrollHuman without rate limiting. A compromised or malicious operator can enroll 30 dummy identities in a single block, flushing the entire root history buffer and invalidating every in-flight proof — a targeted DoS against all active handshakes. Fix: add a per-block enrollment cap (e.g., max 3 enrollments per block via a `lastEnrollBlock` + counter check) and/or switch to a time-weighted eviction policy where roots expire after N blocks rather than N enrollments. This preserves the proof validity window independent of enrollment rate.

title: Nullifiers lack chain ID domain separation enabling cross-chain replay
description: All three circuits compute nullifiers without binding to a chain identifier: HumanUniqueness uses Poseidon(scope, secret), AgentPolicy uses Poseidon(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce), and Delegation uses Poseidon(delegatorCmt, delegateeCmt, nonce). If IdentityRegistry is deployed on multiple chains (e.g., Base + Optimism) with the same Merkle trees, a valid proof on chain A can be submitted on chain B since usedNonces/usedNullifiers are per-contract state. Fix: add a public input `chainId` to each circuit and include it in the nullifier hash (e.g., Poseidon(scope, secret, chainId)). The contract should verify `chainId == block.chainid` in the verification function. This is a one-line circuit change plus contract-side check per circuit.

title: Delegation circuit does not bind delegator signature to session nonce
description: In Delegation.circom, the delegator's EdDSA signature covers the delegation token = Poseidon(delegatorScopeCommitment, delegateeCredCommitment, delegateeScope, delegateeExpiry). The sessionNonce is a separate public input used only for the delegation nullifier, but it is NOT included in the signed message. This means a delegation signature obtained in session S₁ can be reused in session S₂ with a different nonce — the circuit will accept it because the signature is valid and a new (non-replayed) nullifier is produced. The delegator cannot revoke a signed delegation token. Fix: include sessionNonce in the signed delegation token: Poseidon5(delegatorScopeCommitment, delegateeCredCommitment, delegateeScope, delegateeExpiry, sessionNonce). This binds each delegation authorization to exactly one session.
